Sum of squares of achievable cycle lengths in a side-4 triangular grid

An equilateral triangle T of side length 4 is partitioned into unit equilateral triangle cells by drawing lines parallel to its sides. Two cells are adjacent if they share a common edge.

A robot moves from cell to an adjacent cell, and it is not allowed to move to the cell it occupied immediately before (no immediate backtrack).

For a positive integer k, say k is achievable if there exists a closed robot path of length k that visits exactly k distinct cells.

Let S be the set of all achievable k. Compute
    \sum_{k\inS} k^2,
and give the answer as a three-digit zero-padded integer.

Solution.
Step 1 (translate robot motion to cycles; build_dual_adjacency_graph)
Form the adjacency graph G whose vertices are the unit triangle cells and whose edges connect adjacent cells.
A closed path of length k that visits exactly k distinct cells is exactly a simple cycle of length k in G.
So S is the set of cycle lengths that occur as simple cycles in G.

Step 2 (parity restriction from bipartite structure; bipartite_cycle_parity)
Color each unit triangle by its orientation (up-pointing vs down-pointing). Adjacent triangles have opposite orientation, so G is bipartite.
Therefore every cycle in G has even length. In particular, all k\inS are even.

Step 3 (replace cycle classification by a face-enclosure model; convert_cycle_to_enclosed_internal_structure)
View the subdivision of T as a planar triangulation of the big triangle. The graph G is the planar dual: its vertices are the small triangles, and edges record shared sides.

In a planar triangulation, the bounded faces of the dual graph correspond exactly to the interior vertices of the primal triangulation (the triangles around an interior vertex form a cycle in the dual).

For side length 4, the triangular grid has exactly
    (4-1)(4-2)/2 = 3
interior vertices (one in the row at distance 1 from the top side, and two in the next row).
Hence G has exactly 3 bounded faces. Each such face is a 6-cycle in G (six small triangles meet around an interior vertex).

Let these 3 bounded faces be F1,F2,F3.

Step 4 (perimeter formula for unions of bounded faces; enclosed_region_perimeter_relation + compute_k_from_m_e)
Any simple cycle C in a planar graph encloses a (nonempty) set of bounded faces; the cycle C is exactly the boundary of the union of those enclosed faces.

Let the cycle C enclose m of the faces {F1,F2,F3}, and let e be the number of shared edges between the enclosed faces (i.e., the number of edges of G that lie between two enclosed faces and thus disappear from the boundary).
Each face contributes 6 boundary edges, and each shared edge removes 2 boundary edges (counted once from each face), so the boundary length is
    k = 6m - 2e.                                                   (∗)

Moreover, because C is a single simple cycle (one boundary component), the enclosed faces must form a connected union.

Step 5 (finite enumeration on the 3-face internal adjacency graph; enumerate_connected_internal_subgraphs)
For side length 4, the 3 interior vertices form a small triangle in the grid, so the 3 dual faces F1,F2,F3 are pairwise adjacent (each pair shares exactly one dual edge). Thus the "face adjacency graph" on {F1,F2,F3} is a triangle.

Now list all connected subsets of {F1,F2,F3} and compute k using (∗):

- m=1: choose one face. Then e=0, so k=6 \cdot 1-2 \cdot 0=6.

- m=2: choose two faces. Any two are adjacent, so e=1, hence k=6 \cdot 2-2 \cdot 1=10.

- m=3: choose all three faces. All three adjacencies occur, so e=3, hence k=6 \cdot 3-2 \cdot 3=12.

Therefore
    S = {6,10,12}.

(Each is achievable: take the boundary cycle of the chosen union of faces.)

Step 6 (compute requested aggregate; compute_sum)
Compute the required sum of squares:
    \sum_{k\inS} k^2 = 6^2 + 10^2 + 12^2 = 36 + 100 + 144 = 280.
As a three-digit zero-padded integer, this is 280.

Final Answer: 280.

Uniqueness Test

1) The robot condition "closed length k and exactly k distinct cells" forces the path to be a simple cycle of length k in the cell-adjacency graph G. Thus achievable k are exactly the cycle lengths present in G.

2) The bipartition by triangle orientation makes G bipartite, so all cycle lengths are even; this eliminates any odd k a priori.

3) In a planar triangulation, bounded faces of the dual graph correspond bijectively to interior vertices of the primal. For side length 4, the primal grid has exactly 3 interior vertices, so G has exactly 3 bounded faces, each a 6-cycle.

4) Any simple cycle in a planar graph is the boundary of the union of the bounded faces it encloses. Because the boundary is a single simple cycle, the enclosed set of faces must be connected. Hence every cycle length k must arise as the perimeter of a connected union of some of these 3 bounded faces.

5) The internal adjacency among the 3 faces is fixed (a triangle), so the connected face-unions are exactly: 1 face, 2 adjacent faces, or all 3 faces. The perimeter formula k=6m-2e then forces k\in{6,10,12}, and each value is realized by the corresponding boundary cycle. Therefore S is uniquely determined as {6,10,12}.

6) Since S is unique, \sum_{k\inS} k^2 is uniquely 280.

280
